sentences.text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]
My name is Saul Stermer.
S-A-U-L S-T-E-R-M-E-R.
In Europe, in my town, they used to call me shouldn't care
The 4th of May, 1920.
[LAUGHS] 76.
It was Poland.
Like in 1939, we were as Poland.
It's Galicia.
And the most is Ukrainian-- right now it's Ukrainian, mostly Ukrainian people live there.
Korolowka.
Yeah.
K-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] K-O-R-O-L-O-W-K-A, Korolowka.
It was nice.
It was a small town, only about 1,500 people.
It had 1,500 Jews.
Yeah.
And Korolowka was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a very nice place to-- to live there.
I had a lot of friends.
We had organizations for-- for-- Zionist organizations.
There was the Betar, the Gordonia, Shomer, and the [INAUDIBLE].
In our family, we were six children.
Three brothers and three sisters.
And--
I live with my parents, yeah.
My father was a businessman, and we had fields, horses, cows, stables, barns, a big house.
And we have like everything.
My father was a good man.
He-- his pleasure was if he can-- could help somebody.
He was like-- so he was a saint.
He helped a lot of people in his life.
And my mother was a very smart woman.
Very, very--
They call him Seder, but his real Hebrew name was Sabbtai.
Sabbtai.
My mother's name was Esther.
Maiden name, Sternshuss.
She comes in a family from very smart people.
My mother was a homemaker.
She could do everything.
It-- there's not a thing that my mother couldn't do.
And also she had always a business.
She has-- by '39, 1939, by the end, which I remember good, she had a store, they sell flour to people.
And we are good.
I had good friends.
I-- I was-- we had the irons.
We played soccer.
I was the captain for them, and the team.
And not one of my friends are left alive.
They all gone.
Oh, yeah.
I was-- I was always happy.
And also I could do everything.
I was very handy with my hands.
I could do-- I had own-- back home, I had like a place where I had a-- like a place I used to work with wood.
I used to make--
Like a little wood shop.
I could make nice frames, little cassettes, and I even made shoes for the-- for my friends, for the girls like summertime.
Shoes like wooden soles, and the little leather.
I was a-- it was good till 1939.
It was good be-- I belonged to the Betar, and-- and it was good.
But then the war broke out.
It still was-- when the war broke out in 1939, and the 17th of September the Red Army marched into us.
Which we were safe for a while.
Because they-- they-- they were not bad.
They only were bad, the Red Army, for the very rich.
And we were-- we were all right.
Like we had what-- we had our land.
We had our-- but in 1941, on the 22nd of June, when the Russian war broke out between the Russians and Germans, then it start, that in July the Hungarian army-- we were lucky.
The Hungarian army came into us.
They didn't hit nobody.
They didn't kill nobody.
And soon when they left, the Germans came in.
The gestapo.
They-- they were in [INAUDIBLE].
We-- we knew.
Yes, I forgot to mention this here.
In 1939-- 1938, my mother was always-- she-- she was reading of the paper.
My father too, of course.
But my mother had more time.
And she said that we have to run away from here because there's for sure going to be a war.
And we registered to go to Canada.
I don't know if you-- if I mentioned this to you.
And we were accepted.
In order to go to Canada, you had to be like a farmer, and you had to have four working people, and you had to have money to buy a farm and to pay your-- your fare.
We were qualified.
And we-- on the first-- we were supposed to be on the 8th of September, 1939, on the boat to go to Canada.
The place was-- was reserved, and the 1st broke out the war.
Everything was sold.
And we couldn't go.
So we got stuck there.
But to go back, when the-- when the-- when the-- when the Germans occupied us, then it start bad times.
For instance, when-- there was Ukrainian police right away established.
We had to wear those Star of David, when this here, which I never put on.
I never put it on.
Never.
Other than-- I don't know, but I-- I never put it on.
I said, I'm not going to put on this here.
And now it start, they start-- they-- the-- the first summer was not so bad for anyone.
In the fall, they start to grab young boys like all my friends.
Not all my friends, but a lot of my friends.
The Ukrainian police.
And you know, they were there-- there was the-- the Jewish unit out there called.
They had to supply them boys to the labor camp.
And they used to go from house to house, and who they find they took away.
We in our house, as my mother say-- before anything, my mother say, boys, make bunkers.
Make bunkers.
We call in my older brother, which he is a [INAUDIBLE].
That my older brother, he was something that for the family.
Nissen.
And I always say that my parents were blessed to have a son like he, and we were all lucky that we have him for a brother.
Because first the Almighty, and then possible my brother helped us survive.
So my mother says, boys, make bunkers.
And we were very handy.
We made six bunkers.
Where?
We had a lot of barns.
See, we-- we made a bunker in one place, there was a-- a box.
Like-- like-- a box from both hold to keep flour.
He opened the bottom, and we dug a big hole.
And the door was closed.
And then somebody put some-- they didn't discover one never.
So we made those bunkers.
And when they start going around and from house to house, we jumped in the bunkers and they didn't discover us.
How long at a-- one time did you have to stay in the bunkers?
We had to stay for-- sometimes for a day.
So then we see it's very bad.
We can't be in this town.
So my father-- a lot of people knew my father.
He had a lot of connections.
The Germans made some [GERMAN], it's called.
So like-- like-- it was landowners, big farms.
I don't know how to explain this.
Big farm.
Few hundred acres of land, horses.
And they took it over, and they-- they call it-- they gave it a German name, [GERMAN].
So my brother-- my father went there.
And he-- and-- in every [GERMAN], there was a shop that they repair the wagons, this lay wood.
And they also have one like a-- a locksmith.
So he went to this guy that he had the shop on the wood, and his name was-- a Ukrainian guy, very nice man.
[UKRAINIAN].
He said, listen.
I have a-- a son.
He's very handy.
You-- he can do everything.
I want you should keep him here.
Ask the German-- because the German guy there-- ask if it's allowed that you-- he says, yeah.
They-- they didn't pay me nothing.
They didn't give me nothing.
And I was there.
Came down in October.
And I was through the winter there.
I was working there.
The German used to come in.
He looked at me.
And I was working there.
It was very good.
My duties was I-- I used to help-- they repaired wagons.
They are made to make you know, wagon as a part.
A wheel, to repair this.
As I cut wood.
I-- I worked there.
At night, I slept with my-- my aunt still-- my mother's sister.
This was in Bilcze Zlote.
Yeah.
My mother say-- this my mother's town, by she was born.
My mother's sister--
[POLISH].
She was-- one day she was killed.
They-- in that time, the-- the Jews still live in the small villages.
They left, and this 1941, '42 winter, they-- they didn't move nobody yet.
So I slept there.
My brother Nissen also came with me, and he-- since he was very experienced in horses because he was in the Polish cavalry, they also allow him to work with the horses.
So we both worked there all winter.
So we were safe to-- from to go into the concentrate-- into that labor camp, which nobody returned from there alive.
They all gone.
They-- they were labor camps near our area.
There was one, [POLISH], [POLISH], [POLISH], [POLISH].
There was a lot of labor camps they used to-- the Ukrainian police kept those boys and brought them there and finish.
So we were working all winter there.
In the spring, in April, the Germans came out with-- with their tricks, or how you call it.
That no Jew allowed to stay in the small villages.
They all have to go to the city.
So they all have to-- they didn't allow us to stay also in this [GERMAN].
We had to come back to the city.
And it started the same thing.
So you came back to Korolowka?
Came back to Korolowka in April.
They couldn't work there because no Jews are allowed to.
And it started the same thing.
Grabbing to the-- to the camps.
To the labor camps.
So not far from Korolowka was another [GERMAN].
So I went there, and I say, I was working like-- in this-- like a carpenter.
And they hire me there.
They say, OK, work.
Who did you speak to?
German?
There was-- there was a director.
Not a German.
A Polack.
And he says, it's OK.
Because they needed workers.
It was like May, June.
You know, they need the workers.
And they also took some Jewish people, Jewish kids, to work, to help in the fields in-- near there.
So I worked there all summer, 1942.
And at night you came back to your family?
No.
I slept there.
In the [GERMAN].
I had-- in the place where I was working, I made myself a little bed, like a little thing.
And I slept there.
Food, they-- they gave me some food there.
They treat me good because I-- I-- they like me there.
You know, I work good, and-- and it was-- so I was all summer there.
Once they came, they came for the-- because-- for the Jews, a German.
And-- and-- and Ukrainian police.
They came to grab all the Jewish kids were working there, and they caught me too.
This was-- I know exactly what month.
I know what was on the field.
It was about-- by the end of June.
And they brought me to the city.
And this director from the-- from the camp, from the-- I mean, from the camp, from the [GERMAN], he came with me too.
He says, I'm not going to let you take away the-- they-- they should take you away.
Come with me to the-- to the Judenrat.
And he went there and he says, this boy you have to let go.
I need him.
This is the Polish man.
I need them because there is right now harvest, and we have broken wagons and everything.
We need this guy.
They opened a window, and I just jumped out.
They just open a window and I jumped out, and I was saved there.
So now this was going like July, August.
I was working there.
Was everybody in your family safe, relatively safe?
Everybody was safe.
We had a-- if anything happened, we have those bunkers.
So I-- before Sukkot, they were talking already from-- from actions, [GERMAN].
What they-- what that means, it's a German word, action.
They came to the city, and they grab the people and they take them away like to Belzec or somewhere.
So a day before Sukkot-- this was a Friday.
Saturday was the first day of Sukkot in 1942.
I thought, this is yom tov, and I know my mother still have something to-- she-- I'm sure she bake something, she cook something nice.
I want to go home.
I know it's-- it's danger to go home.
So I borrow a burly bag by a guy who worked with me, and I put in small pieces of wood.
Because I knew they-- they need wood to cook.
A big bag of wood.
And I was carrying this on my back.
It was pressing.
But I have to bring home for my mother some wood.
Nine kilometers.
And I was carrying this.
And I come in Friday like-- and my father sees me.
He says, why do you come home?
Any day it's going to be a-- a pogrom here.
They going to-- they going to take you-- it's terrible.
You have a good place.
Why don't you stay there?
Go back.
My mother said, let's-- he came home already.
Let him stay overnight.
In the morning, he's going to go back.
She gave me some challah and-- and-- and nice-- nice stuff.
But nobody slept in the house.
Everybody was sleeping in the bunkers.
And we had a house like in the-- to the market.
And my mother was standing there almost all night listening, if she doesn't hear any noise.
But I had to go back early in the morning.
So early in the morning, I woke up.
Maybe 4 o'clock.
Because I have to leave.
And my mother was working.
Our town was like kind on a little hill.
All around was valleys and a-- and a river.
And it was very nice morning.
Saturday morning.
It was first day of Sukkot.
So my mother walked with me down the hill.
And I tell, Ma, I forgot the bag.
I have to give back the bag.
So she says, stay here.
I'll go back and pack the bag, because there is something that-- if you go back, it's not-- it's not lucky.
So I was-- she brought me the bag.
And as I walked down the hill, and I start cross over that little river, and I walk up-- up another hill to go there.
I hear a shot.
I turn around my head, I see Germans around the city.
See police in this.
And I see people running.
We had a-- a big marketplace.
I see people running like maybe 20, 30 people running this way.
I see they can't run there.
They run back this way.
I look around.
I see what's going on.
So I didn't go to the [GERMAN].
I went straight to the forest.
It was a Saturday.
And I was listening to all day, pow, pow, shooting.
I didn't know what's going on.
And I was sitting in the forest.
And in the evening, I thought I'll go back to the [GERMAN] because I didn't eat nothing all day.
So I go.
While I go back, I come to the road.
Police are driving in a car and start shooting.
And I hear a bullet just pow through-- near my near.
So I-- I didn't go to the-- already to my place.
And I run to the field.
And it was-- it was-- it was not cold.
And it was like those corn stacks.
I made myself a-- like a little house.
And I was lying, and I lie down.
I had that burly bag.
And I was sleeping.
The moon was, I think, a full moon.
And I was lying all night there.
And I was lying all night there.
In the morning, I was going slowly to the-- to the camp-- to the-- the place, the [GERMAN].
And I see the guys.
And I start asking the Ukrainians, what happened?
They went-- oh, he says.
Your parents, they gone.
He says in Ukrainian, [SPEAKING UKRAINIAN].
They took away your whole people, your-- your-- your father and mother.
You know, I start crying.
But then I didn't believe them.
I says, no.
My father and mother, they-- I'm sure they didn't discover our-- our bunkers.
I'm sure not.
And I sent somebody.
And they came to our house and find my mother inside.
No, in the house.
Because the way the German was-- after-- an hour later after the action, you could go walk around.
They didn't need you.
They come next time for you.
So they-- my mother told them that in our bunker, everybody's alive-- everybody survived.
My two-- my mother's two sisters with the kids.
And my sister.
Everybody was in the bunker.
They didn't discover even one bunker.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So it was for me good news.
But it was very sad because they took away half of the town.
But-- at least half of the town.
All my friends, they-- it was terrible.
And then they say-- the Germans came to say that I can't stay no more in this.
I have to go back.
And yet we can't stay more in Korolowka.
All the people, they-- what they remained there.
Another week later, everybody has to go to a ghetto.
Now, my mother says, no, sir.
We are not going to go to the slaughterhouse.
We are not going there.
She says, call in my-- my older son.
And said, Nissen, you-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] you go somewhere in the forest.
Find a place.
Dig holes.
We are not going to the slaughterhouse.
We are not going-- Nissen went to look in the ghetto.
He could-- we didn't have a chance in the ghetto.
We were a family, six children, father, mother, grandchildren.
My father-- my brother-in-law, Fischer Dodek.
If you go, we have no chance.
We-- in the ghetto, you come in.
They give you a room there, and then next day you're gone.
Here, we were in our house.
We-- we survived this-- this terrible thing.
So my brother start looking.
With my father, start looking.
And not far from Bilcze Zlote, because he knew the area good.
So he came-- he came down.
There was in the village was a-- a Jewish doctor, Dr.
[POLISH].
And he had his parents there.
And he also tried to find a place where to hide his parents, because the Ukrainian police give him-- him and his wife a chance.
I think a child they have, too, to stay there.
But the father and the mother and their daughter, they are to go back to [POLISH], to the ghetto.
So in Bilcze Zlote, there was a grotto.
There is a grotto still, because I-- I visit there a few years ago.
But this grotto was a grotto when we-- like tourist attraction.
Kids from the school used to go to visit.
This grotto was clean through corridors and he had a map, and he had also a guide.
So one of the guys, a good friends of this [POLISH], my father says, I think-- No.
I think you should try this-- this place, this grotto.
And we went, and we had a look.
You know, you came in.
This grotto was made like with steps to go down, and a big opening.
You come in, it was very nice.
Winter time, it was warm.
And we went in there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So we didn't go to the ghetto.
So my mother and me and my younger brother and my sister, we all went in.
But I have to stop for a second to go back.
After-- after this when the-- all the Jews had left, had to leave Korolowka, the Germans appointed some Jews, and they get-- they gave them a badge.
But he has to have a horse and a buggy to collect the old stuff.
You know, what's left.
Old-- old rags, and old stuff, [GERMAN].
And you have to bring like the rags and the old steel, the scrap, in a place.
They put in a barn.
They pay for this.
So--
Pardon me?
No.
We paid to get the badge.
They didn't pay you.
So my brother and my father and my-- and my-- my father-in-law, Fischer Dodek, we all three got those badges.
[SPEAKING GERMAN].
Old stuff.
They-- like junk.
To bring the junk together.
And they had a chance.
They had-- everyone had to have a horse and a buggy.
And they-- and they had a chance to stay in our town.
And the families were in the cave.
And they at night used to come, and they bring us food.
And this was 1942, '43.
This year.
And we worked.
We didn't go to the ghetto.
And the ghetto was terrible.
They were killing, and-- I don't have to tell you what it was in the ghettos.
And we were there.
Our family.
The doctor's family.
Was another dentist, [POLISH].
With his family.
We were there all winter.
Not me.
Not me.
My father.
Only at night.
Nobody knew that we are there.
At night, my brother-- not my-- my brother used to come and bring some food.
You have no chance.
You--
So that's where you got your food when you were in the-- in the cave.
In the first cave.
My-- my brother and my brother-in-law, Fischer Dodek, and they had this badges for a while.
This was only till the spring, and then they would-- I think they liquidated us.
So they-- we had a chance to be there, and they brought food.
So my brother used to come almost every second night, and bring some stuff, and bring boards, and bring this, and bring food.
Where did he sleep when he wasn't in the cave?
He was in the town, in Korolowka.
There were a few houses that a few Jews still stayed there.
Now, we knew that this is-- but it was very bad, there in this cave.
There was no water.
See, food you can bring in.
Water, it was very hard.
So there were some places where drops, and we used to put the bottle and get a bottle of water.
But winter time, we had snow.
So we melt the snow.
It was very hard to get water.
And you built fires in the--
Yeah.
We-- and my brother brought in a stove, tin stove.
And we had to cook very far from the place where we were sleeping because the smoke-- once almost my-- my niece, Simha, was almost-- once-- she was out.
Be hard to survive it.
I mean, it was very hard to-- to bring her back alive.
From the smoke.
Then we moved away further, and we moved away further the place where to cook.
But we knew that we are not safe here.
Now, every evening--
